Notes to support status:
• Solution outlined: ML Inference Unikernel for ARM64 .

• Risks acceptable & manageable: Dropped dynamic shapes and full ONNX compliance to manage complexity .

• Pursuit viable: Benchmarks prove unikernels offer 10-50x smaller memory footprints and 40-100x faster boot times.

• Timely feedback provided: Dr. Romi Banerjee and TA Sumeet S Patil provided guidance on requirements and architecture .

• Changes communicated: SRS updated to V2.0 based on feedback.

• Requirements shared & priorities clear: SRS v2.0 establishes Critical to Low priorities for FRs, PRs, and PDRs .

• Conflicts addressed: Excluded general-purpose OS features and dynamic shapes to ensure coherent edge-inference focus .

• Team knows what to deliver: Traceability matrix established.

• Architecture selected: 5-layer unikernel architecture chosen .

• Critical interfaces demonstrated: UART protocol implemented, tested, and validated with a test harness .

• System exercised: Benchmarking framework (Unikraft vs Linux) built and performance measured.

• Works as one unit: 4-member team has clear, non-overlapping responsibilities (Architecture, Integration, ML Runtime, System Components) .

• Communication open: GitHub repository and Trello Kanban boards utilized for continuous coordination.

• Development started: Coding active on baremetalOS and UART_Implementation branches .

• Progress monitored: Trello used for sprint planning and task tracking .

• Results achieved: UART tests passing and benchmarking trials concluded.

• Practices & tools in use: C11, ARM64 assembly, GCC 10+, QEMU 6.0+, CMake, and GitHub actively utilized by the whole team .

• Foundation established: Standardized function naming patterns and error handling conventions applied to codebases.

Goal: Integrate the feat/onnx ML runtime with the stable Kernel_API.

Key Tasks: Link the ONNX parser/executor with the kernel's memory manager (KMEM_TensorAlloc) and cooperative scheduler (THREAD_Yield).

Success Criteria: A single QEMU binary builds without errors, printing the ML runtime ready status.

Goal: First successful end-to-end inference run in QEMU.

Key Tasks: Implement UART binary protocol (FR-021/022) to receive a simple ONNX model (e.g., MNIST) and return results. Connect the UART driver to the integrated runtime.

Success Criteria: Host PC sends model → MiniOS infers → returns the correct digit via UART. Measured latency matches the Kernel_API timer demonstration.

Goal: Validate core NEON operators (Conv2D, MatMul) for correctness and measure performance against SRS PR-001.

Key Tasks: Port NEON operator implementations from feat/onnx to use Kernel_API services. Run benchmarks with varying tensor sizes.

Success Criteria: Operator outputs match reference implementations (e.g., Python ONNX Runtime). Inference throughput is within 20% of the projected bare-metal target.

Goal: Prepare a demonstrable MVP for stakeholders (Dr. Romi Banerjee).

Key Tasks: Create a simple host-side Python script that sends a model, receives results, and reports latency. Document the build and run process.

Success Criteria: Stakeholders can clone the repo, run make qemu, and see a successful inference with printed results and timing.

Progress: Development now focused on feat/onnx branch integration and UART protocol.

Progress Monitored: Trello board updated with tasks for ONNX integration, UART protocol, and operator validation.

Results Achieved: Kernel_API branch completed with all 23 assertions passing; stable ABI established for ML runtime.
